posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
The Rousseau price is of course way under market. That being said, if I could buy at that price for consumption only, I am not sure I would bite.

The quoted price of Rousseau 2018 at CA$1270 - is very similar in Ontario and in Quebec. They are institution of the Provincial Governments and not private Company. For the last many years, the customers are only allowed to buy one bottle in a lottery system.

In the good old days, you are allowed to buy as many as you wish. The question of whether one should drank it or not - I better let Michael answer your question

For me, I let other burgundy geeks from Singapore, Hong Kong and Shanghai enjoy them.

My most recently preferred and favorite producer for Chambertin and CdBeze is the one and the only Mr. Chambertin - the young Pierre Damoy who sells grapes from CdBese to many ohher vignerons.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: BTW although I don’t think beze is an inferior vineyard my post was directed towards only straight Chambertin because there are fewer producers.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
BTW although I don’t think beze is an inferior vineyard my post was directed towards only straight Chambertin because there are fewer producers.

Michael …what is your meaning of saying : *only straight Chambertin

If the bottle is labelled as : Appellation Chambertin Controlled or Appellation Chambertin Grand-Cru Controlled - the AC laws allowed the vignerons to included juice from the lier-dite : Chambertin and/or lieu-dite Clos de Beze.

In other words, there is only straight : beze …legally speaking of course.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Michael…the only way to find out who would be your preferred producers in Chambertin …is to have a long term plan.

May I suggest the followings : for vintage 2019 - try to buy all the Chambertin available in the market and to be sure at least mutiplce bottle of minimum of 6 of each - including : Leory, Rousseau…etc…etc…

Then do a horizon tasting of all them ( blind )…when they are 4 or 5 years old ; then at age 8 ( mostly likely closed-down for most of them all of then) and for the 3rd time at 10…etc…etc…

If you do not own too many bottles from Leroy. I would suggest…to skip the Leroy ( no body wish to drink a Leroy Chambertin knowing well it may closd-down )

I remember when I was in the ParkerB…there was a poster there who bought all the Montrachet ( in a given vintage year - which I forget what year ).

That is what I called a burgundy geek !!

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Michael…thanks for the additional comments.

My joke regarding the strait Chambertin is just a joke. Hope you are not offended…as I know what you really meant

In the early 1990s, I did the exercise of my suggested long term plan - including Leroy which is normally one bottle for 4to 5 bottles of the others : Rousseau, Trapet and R-T.

My conclusion was exact like yours : Rousseau; Tapet; R-T and Prieur in descending order. Leroy …while at least was on-bar but the price is too much for what was inside the bottle. At the beginning of 2000s the Rousseau went up drastically in the secondary market. So…i settled down on buying only R-T and Prieur for my own consumption.

Lately in the last 10 years, my taste changed and also due to the fact the new Pierre has taken over the Domaine Damoy and also thanks to Bill Nanson’s Book, I switch to Pierre Damoy. That being said : I would like to add R-T ( in Quebec Canada in my view ) is the best QPR for Chambertin.

I still own the followings from vintage 2005 :

Prieur - Chambertin 05 (1029-4153 $223)
Rossignol-Trapet - Chambertin 05 (1082-6287 $142)

Alan Weinberg:

a name not mentioned: Damoy

It’s not mentioned for the obvious reasons.

sorry but Damoy since 1993 does a fine job w their Chambertins.

Alan, what is a “fine job” in this context? A decent pinot? Sure. A wine that makes you think it is made in one of the best climats in all of Burgundy? Certainly not.


I think I’m the person (or at least one of them) that’s indirectly responsible for this thread because I’ve posted several times that Chambertin is the most overrated Grand Cru in Burgundy. To be clear, I don’t think that about the terroir, but because of who’s making the wines. When we talk about great “Le Chambertin” we’re really talking about Rousseau and, for the last few years, Trapet. Otherwise…boy, the pickings for great wines are slim*! (And I’ve been curious enough to ask this question to producers in Burgundy.) The claim on this forum is often made that Burgundy is about “producer over terroir”. In that case, where does that leave Chambertin? Basically it’s an overrated grand cru because there are almost no great winemakers making wine there. When people say “Chambertin is great”, what they really mean is that Rousseau makes great wine there.
To put it another way, what’s better terroir, La Tache or Grand Rue? I’m certainly not qualified to answer, but we might feel differently if DRC owned Grand Rue and Lamarche owned La Tache. If they switched ownership of the monopoles tomorrow, would everyone still take the view La Tache is better? Would the wine made from La Tache still be better? Or do people think La Tache is the best terroir in Burgundy because of who’s been making the wine all these years?


*Compare this to, say, Musigny, where even producers whom no one considers among the true elite (Prieur, Jadot, Drouhin) make some spectacular wines.

I think I heard that Napoleon agreed…

Napoleon used to cut it with water, however, affronting the Bourgeois sensibilities of Talleyrand in the process.

Come one, you know better, Talleyrand was a bishop, aristocrat, prince and diplomat… with exquisite tastes who loved good food, fine wine indeed (Haut Brion his favorite) and women (many… some insinuate even his niece…who knows?..) not really a Bourgeois in my book… He housed the Spanish royal family (at Napoleon request) in his chateau de Valencay who really enjoyed their short stay (excellent goat cheese in Valencay by the way…)

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
I love Bichot ( Clos Frantin ) wines but sadly I only bought his lower-ends red and I love all them as the style fitted my taste. Sadly I have never even see a bottle of Clos Frantin Chambertin - despite he owns landing there ( 0.17 ha around the same size as Bouchard and Mortet).

Peter - for Burgundy lovers, nobody beats nobody up. We - quite often - agree to disagree . Once in a while, we fight but all with good reasons.

Wine is a genteel hobby, pursued mostly by happy lushes. But the corkscrews stab for the jugular when wine in the decanter is a red Burgundy which is made from the fragile, ornery, and thus quintessential French pinot noir grape - cause acrimony because Rorschach wines, taking the measure of your inner hedonist. How you feel about them is really a statement of first principles, about what kind of satisfaction you believe wine should deliver; immediate and obvious ( Rousseau come to my mind and it is also there from first drop the last and which I agree totally with Greg ) or gradual and subtle ( Damon comes to my mind ).

The last paragraph of my post is a copy of the first paragraph of an article by Mike Steinberger on 2003/04/04 in Slate under the title : The Burgundy Brawl - Wines that can start a fight.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Machael…Bouchard owns 0.15 ha in the Chambertin and none in CdBeze. So his Chambertin should be domaine. It could be confirmed : if you take a closer look at the label. There should be the key word : Domaine printed in the middle part of the label.

Bouchard gets the grapes from Damoy’s s Beze. Damoy owns 5.36 out of 15.46 ha of CdBeze. The grapes were from 1920 - 2000.

Damoy once in a while released 4 kinds of CdBeze under the lebel : LaCabane ( young vins 2000); CdBzee; CdBeze v.v. and CdBeze - Reserve ( grapes 1920). He only released one kind of Chambertin with the name : straightly Chambertin ( with no juice or grapes from his CdBese).

I only drank his CdBeze and his Chambertin. I wish I have a chance to try his 3 other kinds of CdBeze.

posts.name: Ryan Wihera
posts.cooked:
Don’t beat me up for asking, but… What do folks think about the Clos Frantin Chambertin? Not looking for a Rousseau/Trapet experience, but is it of reasonable grand cru quality? Picked up a bottle of the 2014 for a tiny fraction of price - decent value play?
Thanks,
Peter

2014 Chambertin/Gevrey Chambertin wines are really great classic “Burgundy lovers’ Burgundy”. With the vintage being typically expressed as a less dense one, the 2014’s got poor press and scores, relatively, early on. However, I think a lot of people are finding that Cote de Nuits reds are absolutely stunning right now, after some of that edge has tapered off. It was a vintage overshadowed by 2015 and as such that’s why you can find some insane deals on great wines. That should be a really great bottle and definitely a great value play.

Semi-unrelated note I had a 2014 Savigny-les-Beaune “Les Lavieres” from Marchand Tawse a few weeks ago and it handily showed up someone else’s 2015 Corton that I’d had recently, in both elegance, aromatics, and pure tastiness. Definitely a sleeper vintage for reds (the whites were so good that everyone drank them years ago, I feel like!) that is rewarding patience now.

P.S. Marchand Tawse makes an excellent Clos de Beze if you can find it, not quite as dense as Rousseau can be but depends on the experience you’re looking for.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Not based on the prices I’m seeing and paying. I don’t buy Trapet so it’s possible that you are getting a normal retail markup and the prices I’m seeing are higher.

That the open market is pricing the “inferior” grand crus higher is telling you something, I think.

Yes, but surely more about producer than vineyard.

Of course. But this has always been my point about Chambertin - if Burgundy is all about “producer, producer, producer” (which I think is true), Chambertin just doesn’t compare well. Even to small grand crus like Griotte.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
That the open market is pricing the “inferior” grand crus higher is telling you something, I think.

Yes, but surely more about producer than vineyard.

Of course. But this has always been my point about Chambertin - if Burgundy is all about “producer, producer, producer” (which I think is true), Chambertin just doesn’t compare well. Even to small grand crus like Griotte.

Well, Leroy, Dujac, Dugat-Py, Rousseau, Mortet sounds like a pretty good line up to me.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Yes, but surely more about producer than vineyard.

Of course. But this has always been my point about Chambertin - if Burgundy is all about “producer, producer, producer” (which I think is true), Chambertin just doesn’t compare well. Even to small grand crus like Griotte.

Well, Leroy, Dujac, Dugat-Py, Rousseau, Mortet sounds like a pretty good line up to me.

Leroy and Dugat-Py productions are extremely small, and I’m not a big fan of the Mortet wines. And I believe Chambertin is one is the biggest grand crus. The ratio isn’t great.

I asked this question to a few producers in Burgundy a few months back (who makes a good Chambertin besides Rousseau) and they all struggled to come up with an answer. One laughed and said Leroy, acknowledging my point before I made it.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Of course. But this has always been my point about Chambertin - if Burgundy is all about “producer, producer, producer” (which I think is true), Chambertin just doesn’t compare well. Even to small grand crus like Griotte.

Well, Leroy, Dujac, Dugat-Py, Rousseau, Mortet sounds like a pretty good line up to me.

Leroy and Dugat-Py productions are extremely small, and I’m not a big fan of the Mortet wines. And I believe Chambertin is one is the biggest grand crus. The ratio isn’t great.

I asked this question to a few producers in Burgundy a few months back (who makes a good Chambertin besides Rousseau) and they all struggled to come up with an answer. One laughed and said Leroy, acknowledging my point before I made it.

Well, I took your post above about the hyphenated grand crus to mean that you think the market is pretty good about sniffing out unwarranted pricing disparities. I agree that the market for these things being fairly efficient. So when I look at the prices of the five producers listed above, it’s hard for me to square it with the notion that there isn’t good Chambertin. You could also take a look at how Leroy prices her Chambertin relative to her other grand crus, or Dujac, or the same with Ponsot, and even if you don’t like those producers you still would come to the conclusion that they think pretty highly of their Chambertin.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Well, Leroy, Dujac, Dugat-Py, Rousseau, Mortet sounds like a pretty good line up to me.

Leroy and Dugat-Py productions are extremely small, and I’m not a big fan of the Mortet wines. And I believe Chambertin is one is the biggest grand crus. The ratio isn’t great.

I asked this question to a few producers in Burgundy a few months back (who makes a good Chambertin besides Rousseau) and they all struggled to come up with an answer. One laughed and said Leroy, acknowledging my point before I made it.

Well, I took your post above about the hyphenated grand crus to mean that you think the market is pretty good about sniffing out unwarranted pricing disparities. I agree that the market for these things being fairly efficient. So when I look at the prices of the five producers listed above, it’s hard for me to square it with the notion that there isn’t good Chambertin. You could also take a look at how Leroy prices her Chambertin relative to her other grand crus, or Dujac, or the same with Ponsot, and even if you don’t like those producers you still would come to the conclusion that they think pretty highly of their Chambertin.

I think those prices have more to do with the quantity than the quality, which isn’t rare at the high end of Burgundy. Dujac, for example, makes a tiny amount of Chambertin, but I have a very hard time believing it’s twice as good as Rousseau’s. Same story as the pricing on Roumier’s Echezeaux and Faiveley’s Musigny. I also don’t think those producers (other than Leroy) would claim the Chambertin is their best wine, despite the lofty price - which is kind of my point

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Yes, but surely more about producer than vineyard.

Of course. But this has always been my point about Chambertin - if Burgundy is all about “producer, producer, producer” (which I think is true), Chambertin just doesn’t compare well. Even to small grand crus like Griotte.

Perhaps in many cases, but I believe it holds true with Leroy, where the Chambertin may arguably be her finest wine.
Obviously, she believes so as well, as it (along with her Musigny) are priced 20-30% above her other GCs.

Complex question. She shows the most emotion talking about the RSV, and on some level I think that’s her favorite. I think she admires the Musigny the most (“this might be the greatest grand cru”, was what she said last month when we tasted the 2019). But Chambertin is very important to her, as the 1955 Maison Leroy Chambertin is her declared inspiration for the wines she tries to make at Domaine Leroy, and she worked the 1955 vintage at the Domaine des Comtes Saint Quentin, who were important proprietors in Gevrey (they owned the parcel of CdB that went to Clair Dau, then divided between Jadot and Bruno Clair).

posts.name: Peter Chiu

I am with Andrew here.

Ponsot (Chezeaux ) owns only 0.20 ha in Chambertin. If Ponsot did release Chambertin-CdBeze the most likely source ( and logically speaking ) …should be from Damoy.

There is another issue : it is allowed - by laws - to add the from the grapes from lieu-dite Clos de Beze to their juice from Chambertin and label it as such : Chambertin AC.

Chambertin Grand Cru - Although the domaine records indicate a wine made in Chambertin as early as 1969,[14] the current parcel is farmed under the 1982 Metayage Agreement with the Mercier family. These holdings are only 9 rows of vines.[6]
Grape variety: Pinot noir
Vineyard holding: 0.2 hectares (0.49 acres)
Estate bottling: 1969
Planting date: 1955
2008 production: 260.25 liters (274 bottles, 36 magnums, 3 jeroboams, 2 mathusalems)[14] *****

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hallo William,

You mentioned that NEAL MARTIN in his latest report on Burgundy brought the cat out of the bag concerning Charles Van Caneyt and domaine des Chezeaux. I reread that report and found nothing…
Eagerly awaiting your say on the latest vintage there !
SINCERELY JOHAN

You will find Neal’s comments under the subheading “Shifting Sands”, in the second paragraph of that section. Just below, there is also a large picture of Charles, which is accompanied by the caption “Charles van Canneyt’s acquisition of Domaine des Chezeaux will see him farm some of the Côte d’Or’s most desirable vineyards.”

links.title: Sublime Chambertin Burgundy Wine: “I Forget The Name Of The Place; I Forget The Name Of The Girl; But The Wine Was Chambertin” - Quill & Pad
